_meta.caveat: ‘fl.’ = the span of dated catalog records matching the name forms as author/recipient. Outlier years usually mean a retrospective mention, a later copy, a homonym, or a record whose own date carries a low confidence flag — check the records, not this line. Counts are of DISTINCTIVE name forms; bare family surnames (Argüello, Carrillo, Castro, Estrada…) are deliberately left unattributed rather than guessed.

role: First governor of the Californias, 1767–1770; commander of the 1769 expedition.
note: No author/recipient hits: his term predates the dense part of the catalog (C-A 1 is a sparse survey). Retained for completeness.

role: Military commander 1770–74; governor 1782–1791.
Comandante of California 1774–1777; killed at the Colorado in the 1781 Yuma revolt.
note: Matched on the full surname only; bare “Rivera” is ambiguous.

role: Governor 1775–1782; founded San José & Los Angeles; author of the 1779 Reglamento.
Governor 1791–1792; died in office at Monterey.
Acting governor 1792–94 & 1800–04, proprietary 1804–1814; earlier lieutenant-governor at Loreto; died at Soledad 1814.
Governor 1794–1800; founder of Villa de Branciforte.
Last Spanish governor, 1815–1822.
note: Deaccented 'sola' can collide with 'Soledad' fragments; matched as a standalone name.

role: SF comandante; first Mexican-era governor 1822–1825; son of José Darío.
Governor 1825–1831; author of the pioneering 1831 secularization decree.
Governor 1831, deposed; opponent of Echeandía's secularization.
note: 'Victoria' also appears as a first name and in ship names — counts approximate.

role: Governor 1833–1835; the gradualist secularization reglamento; died in office.
Governor 1836–1842 (the 1836 revolution).
note: ⚠ Early hits (1780s–1800s) are earlier Alvarados — including Sgt. Juan Bautista Alvarado, the governor’s grandfather. The governor’s own records begin in the late 1820s.

role: Governor 1842–1845; expelled after the Cahuenga campaign.
Last Mexican governor, 1845–46; earlier Diputación vocal.
Militia commander (San Pasqual, 1846); Pío's brother.
Viceroy of New Spain 1794–1798; namesake of the Villa.
note: Matches include the Villa de Branciforte — the count mixes both; the entry exists to distinguish them.

role: Viceroy of New Spain 1803–1808.
Soldier-officer: SF & Monterey comandante, LA founding comisionado (1786 titles), interim governor 1814–15; father of Luis & Santiago.
note: ⚠ 'José Argüello' in Spanish-era records = José Darío. In 1840s San Diego records, 'José Ramón Argüello' is a DIFFERENT man — see that entry.

role: San Diego comandante, then Sub-Prefect (took charge Jul 1845); son of José Darío.
note: ⚠ 1845–46 San Diego: 'S. Argüello' = Santiago (Sub-Prefect); 'José R. Argüello' = José Ramón (judge). The two sign similar documents — check the office named (adjudicated in the C-A 42 catalog).

role: San Diego alcalde/judge, elected Dec 1844; took the Sub-Prefecture Apr 1846.
note: ⚠ Not Santiago — see the Santiago Argüello entry.

role: Santa Bárbara comandante 1784–1802.
San Diego comandante 1793–1799.
SF/Monterey officer & habilitado; comandante roles through the 1790s; d. 1800.
note: Deaccented ' sal ' is matched as a standalone word to avoid 'salinas' etc.

role: Officer: Monterey, Santa Bárbara, San Diego comandancias (fl. 1780s–1800s).
note: One of several prominent Carrillos — see also Anastasio & Carlos Antonio.

role: Santa Bárbara officer & administrator (fl. 1820s–40s).
Rival 'governor' 1837–38 in the sur-norte conflict; brother of José Antonio.
query: carlos antonio carrillo|carlos ant. carrillo|carlos carrillo

role: LA politician; 1840 Santa Bárbara conspiracy; exiled 1845.

canonical: Guerra y Noriega, José de la
role: Santa Bárbara captain & patriarch (fl. 1800s–40s).
note: ⚠ 'Ministro de (la) Guerra' = the War Ministry, NOT this family — excluded from the count.

role: Monterey alférez/habilitado (fl. 1790s–1810s); executor of Arrillaga's estate.
note: The Estradas of Monterey recur across generations; forms with bare 'Estrada' left unattributed.
query: jose mariano estrada|jose ma estrada|jose m. estrada

role: Monterey prefect (interim, 1841) — the 1841 prefecture proclamations.
Monterey comandante (fl. 1800s–20s).
San Diego official (fl. 1820s–40s); treasurer, juez.
SF comandante & habilitado (fl. 1820s–30s); handed Mission San Rafael's 1836 inventory to John Reed.
Founding-era sergeant→lieutenant; Santa Bárbara company commander; the 1783 LA muster rolls.
query: jose francisco ortega|jose fco. ortega|jose fco ortega

role: San Diego official, 1830s–40s — the 1839 'robber bands' report.
San Diego comandante (fl. 1800s–1820s).
query: francisco maria ruiz|francisco ma ruiz|fran.co ma ruiz

role: LA alcalde (fl. 1820s–40s), repeatedly embattled.
SF-company officer; commander of the failed first Estanislao expedition (1829).
note: 'José Sánchez' forms may also catch a San Fernando friar (Fr. José Sánchez) — friar hits carry 'Fr.'

role: Governor's secretary, comandante, and California's first printer (fl. 1825–42).
Government secretary under Alvarado & Micheltorena; the C-A 35 secretariat hand.
note: 'Jimeno' alone can also be Fr. José Joaquín or Fr. Antonio Jimeno (Santa Bárbara friars) — friar hits carry 'Fr.'

role: LA merchant, alcalde, prefect (fl. 1830s–40s).
note: Distinct from Felipe Tapia (a 1780s San José poblador).

role: LA alcalde & ranchero (fl. 1830s–40s).
Soldier-settler (fl. 1780s–1810s): San José water-works, Branciforte; father of M.G.
Sonoma comandante-general of the northern frontier; the 1829 Estanislao campaign report; the 1833 Ross intelligence.
query: mariano guadalupe vallejo|mariano g. vallejo|m. g. vallejo|m.g. vallejo

role: San José cabo/sergeant & comisionado (fl. 1780s–1800s).
Comandante-general in the 1836 revolution & 1846 (Gavilán); prefect.
note: 'José Castro' is common — 1780s–90s hits are other men (e.g., José Tiburcio Castro's family at San José).

role: SF habilitado/officer, 1790s.
note: Distinct from José Fernández del Campo (1820s Monterey artillery) — separate entry.

role: Monterey artillery commander, late 1820s.
English merchant; Visitador General of the missions 1839–40 — the administrator instructions.
Franciscan father-president 1769–1784.
Father-president 1785–1803; the ~1790 mission-property table; the 1797–98 foundings.
Father-president/prefect (fl. 1810s–1846); the secularization consultations.
Zacatecan comisario-prefecto; first Bishop of the Californias (arr. 1841, d. 1846).
